description: GPU-native PTQ using three frameworks: (1) ONNX Runtime GPU with QDQ format and 3 calibration methods, (2) TensorRT INT8 engine with entropy histogram calibration and layer fusion, (3) PyTorch CUDA: dynamic PTQ + PT2E Inductor INT8.

metric_definitions.accuracy: Top-1 classification accuracy on CIFAR-10 test set (10,000 images)
metric_definitions.params_total: Total parameter count (integer); same architecture → same count across backends
metric_definitions.size_disk_mb: Serialised model file size on disk in megabytes
metric_definitions.latency_mean_ms: Mean batch inference time in milliseconds (GPU: CUDA events; ORT: wall-clock)
metric_definitions.latency_std_ms: Standard deviation of batch inference time in milliseconds
metric_definitions.flops_total: Total floating-point operations = 2 × MACs. IDENTICAL for INT8 and FP32 — quantization does NOT change op count.

ptq_math.quantize: Q(x) = clip(round(x / S) + Z, q_min, q_max)

gpu_advantage_clarification.correct_framing: INT8 does NOT reduce FLOPs. The GPU speedup is a hardware property: INT8 Tensor Core lanes process 4× more elements per cycle than FP32.
gpu_advantage_clarification.memory_bandwidth_reduction: ~4× (1 byte INT8 vs 4 bytes FP32 per weight)

flops_methodology.formula_conv: FLOPs = 2 × (Cin/groups) × Cout × Kh × Kw × Hout × Wout
flops_methodology.critical_note: FLOPs are IDENTICAL for INT8 and FP32 models. Use measured wall-clock latency or throughput (imgs/sec) for actual speedup.

baseline_fp32_gpu.flops_note: FLOPs are IDENTICAL for INT8 and FP32 — quantization changes the storage dtype and enables wider SIMD execution but does NOT reduce the arithmetic operation count. Use inference_latency.mean_ms for real-world GPU speedup comparison.

results.description: ONNX Runtime static INT8 PTQ (calibration=MinMax). QDQ format: QuantizeLinear/DequantizeLinear pairs wrap each op. S = (x_max − x_min) / (2^8 − 1),  Z = round(−x_min / S). Calibrated on 1024 CIFAR-10 training images. GPU inference via CUDAExecutionProvider.

quantization_math.S: S = (x_max − x_min) / (2^8 − 1)   [255 levels for UINT8 activations]
quantization_math.Q(x): Q(x) = clip(round(x / S) + Z, 0, 255)  → stored as UINT8
quantization_math.x\u0302: x̂ = S · (Q(x) − Z)  → reconstructed at op boundary
quantization_math.\u03b5_bound: |ε| ≤ S/2  (smaller S ↔ finer quantisation grid ↔ less error)
quantization_math.format: QDQ (QuantizeLinear / DequantizeLinear node pairs, ONNX opset 10+)

results.ops_quantized: Conv, MatMul, Gemm, Add (residual) — subject to graph partitioner
results.flops_note: FLOPs are IDENTICAL for INT8 and FP32 — quantization changes the storage dtype and enables wider SIMD execution but does NOT reduce the arithmetic operation count. Use inference_latency.mean_ms for real-world GPU speedup comparison.

Requires PyTorch >= 2.3.

framework_notes.onnxruntime_gpu: Requires onnxruntime-gpu. CUDAExecutionProvider dispatches to cuDNN INT8 (QLinearConv), cuBLASLt INT8 (QLinearMatMul). QDQ format.
framework_notes.tensorrt: Highest throughput for production GPU serving. Kernel autotuning at build time (GPU-specific binary — NOT portable). IInt8EntropyCalibrator2.
framework_notes.torch_pt2e: PT2E: torch.export → quantizer annotations → prepare_pt2e → calibrate → convert_pt2e → torch.compile(inductor). Still maturing on CUDA (torch 2.3–2.5).
framework_notes.torch_dynamic_gpu: Stores Linear weights as INT8 but dequantizes to FP16 before cuBLAS GEMM. Saves memory bandwidth, NOT compute.
framework_notes.flops_vs_throughput: FLOPs (op count) are IDENTICAL for INT8 and FP32. The INT8 speedup is entirely a hardware throughput property.
